summary: Matt Rocklin and Joris Van den Bossche dramatically accelerate Python's geospatial analysis stack by rewriting GeoPandas with Cython and adding Dask parallelization. The Cython rewrite provides 10-100x speedups by storing direct pointers to GEOS C objects instead of wrapping them in Python Shapely objects, achieving performance parity with PostGIS. Adding Dask parallelization provides an additional 3-4x speedup on multi-core machines. They demonstrate the improvement by reducing a 3-hour NYC taxi analysis to 8 minutes, though the work remains experimental and they actively seek contributors.
key_points: GeoPandas was slow because it wrapped each geometry in a Shapely Python object and iterated through them in Python, causing both iteration overhead and memory bloat
Cythonizing GeoPandas to directly loop over GEOS pointers achieved 10-100x speedups, matching PostGIS performance since both use the same underlying GEOS library
Dask-geopandas enables parallel processing across cores by partitioning data either naively (by row chunks) or spatially (by geographic region)
Data ingestion via Fiona/GDAL remains a bottleneck now that computation is fast - loading CSV files took half the total runtime in their demo
The work requires upstream Pandas changes to support custom block types for properly tracking GEOS geometry pointers
money_quotes: Python's Geospatial stack is slow. We accelerate the GeoPandas library with Cython and Dask. Cython provides 10-100x speedups.
So instead of using a Pandas object-dtype column that holds shapely objects we instead store a NumPy array of direct pointers to the GEOS objects.
Cythonized GeoPandas and PostGIS run at almost exactly the same speed. This is because they use the same underlying C library, GEOS.
Cities are now doing a wonderful job publishing data into the open. This provides transparency and an opportunity for civic involvement to help analyze, understand, and improve our communities.
The Python GIS stack now has a full-speed library that operates as fast as any other open GIS system is likely to manage.
I suspect that we can build an intuitive and performant parallel GIS analytics system with modest effort.
We believe that this project can have a large social impact and are enthusiastic about pursuing it in the future.
key_insight: By eliminating Python object overhead through Cython and adding Dask parallelization, Python's geospatial stack can match the performance of established tools like PostGIS while maintaining Python's accessibility and ecosystem benefits.
